stories.title: CMBS Special Servicing Rate Reverses May’s Monthly Decline
stories.excerpt: The Trepp CMBS special servicing rate increased by 34 basis points in June to 11.20%, reversing May’s decline. Special servicing rates increased in four of the six major property types in June, reported Trepp. Lodging posted the largest increase, rising 44 bps to 8.89%, and office climbed 36 bps to 17.11%, also led by several large transfers. Mixed-use rose 28 bps to 11.90%, and industrial edged up nine bps to 1.37% Multifamily posted the biggest improvement, falling 27 bps to 8.23%, aided by the large Yorkshire & Lexington Towers loan return. Retail edged down five bps to 12.95%. In June, new transfers totaled roughly $3.08 billion across 42 loans. The largest loan to transfer in June was the $975-million IMC Portfolio loan, which transferred for imminent balloon/maturity default. It’s backed by a 16-property, 9.6-million-square-foot showroom portfolio in Las Vegas and High Point, NC, with properties constructed between 1907 and 2017. The post CMBS Special Servicing Rate Reverses May’s Monthly Decline appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Multifamily special servicing rate fell 27 bps to 8.23% in June, best-performing property type.
Office special servicing jumped 36 bps to 17.11%; lodging rose 44 bps to 8.89%.
Overall CMBS distress rate climbed 34 bps to 11.20%; $3.08B transferred across 42 loans.
stories.summary: CMBS special servicing rates deteriorated in June across most property types, with the overall rate rising 34 bps to 11.20%. Office and lodging saw the sharpest increases (36 and 44 bps respectively), while multifamily improved 27 bps to 8.23%, aided by a large loan return. June transfers totaled $3.08 billion across 42 loans, including a $975 million showroom portfolio loan in Las Vegas and High Point.
stories.impact: Multifamily's improved special servicing trajectory signals relative resilience compared to office and hospitality, but persistent distress across $3.08B in June transfers suggests capital markets remain selective. Developers should monitor refinancing windows narrowing for stressed properties and expect continued pressure on marginal assets, particularly in secondary markets.

stories.title: Return to Lender: Week of July 16, 2026
stories.excerpt: A speculative office building in Atlanta’s West Midtown failed to land a tenant amid broader leasing challenges in the area, forcing the owner to hand over the keys. The Atlanta Business Chronicle reported that Bank OZK took ownership of the vacant 14-story, 225,000-square-foot building called 1050 Brickworks through a deed in lieu of foreclosure, according to property records. Chicago-based Sterling Bay took out an $85.5-million loan with Bank OZK in September 2022 for the building’s construction. Asana Partners, a Charlotte-based real estate firm that maintains ownership of a separate office and retail complex next to 1050 Brickworks, was a limited partner on the deal. Two of the four pari passu pieces of Bank of America Plaza ($400.0 million | multiple conduits | CMBX.8), backed by a Los Angeles ofice property, reported the loan’s liquidation this month, clocking in at a 44% loss severity. The GC25 piece took a loss of $48.4 million, which completely wrote down Classes F and G and partially wrote down Class E. The GC26 piece reported a loss of $10.3 million, which wrote off the remainder of Class H and part of Class G. Three years after foreclosing on a North Broad Street apartment building, its debt holders have sold the property to a national investment firm for $40.5 million, according to the Philadelphia Business Journal. An entity tied to Kayne Anderson Real Estate acquired the 265-unit Lofts 640 at 630-40 N. Broad St. from a CMBS trust that owned the $46-million loan that previously backed the building. The loan matured in late 2021, and the trust took possession of Lofts 640 after a foreclosure judgment was issued on the property in March 2023, according to CMBS reports. The leasehold interest in an office property in Alexandria, VA’s Old Town North sold Wednesday for $9.05 million following a multiday online receivership auction, reported the Washington Business Journal. Spanning 78,381 square feet across four levels, 11 Canal Center Plaza is
bullets: Sterling Bay's Atlanta office project (225K sf, $85.5M loan) surrendered to Bank OZK via deed in lieu.
Philadelphia's 265-unit Lofts 640 sold for $40.5M (after $46M foreclosure), 12% loss on loan.
LA office tower at Bank of America Plaza liquidated at 44% loss severity; CMBS distress broadening.
stories.summary: A speculative 225,000-square-foot office building in Atlanta's West Midtown returned to Bank OZK through foreclosure after failing to secure tenants. Simultaneously, Philadelphia's 265-unit Lofts 640 apartment building sold at a $5.5M loss after foreclosure, and a Los Angeles office property liquidated with 44% loss severity, signaling widening CMBS distress across property types and geographies.
stories.impact: Atlanta West Midtown's office failure underscores weak leasing demand in that submarket, but the Philadelphia apartment liquidation at 87% of prior loan value suggests rental properties are weathering the cycle better than office. Developers should avoid speculative office in secondary markets (Atlanta, second-tier submarkets) but can identify acquisition opportunities in foreclosed multifamily, particularly Class B/C buildings where rents have stabilized.

stories.title: Santa Monica, elevated: Related sets new standard for SoCal luxury with lifestyle amenities, Thermador appliances
stories.excerpt: Today’s luxury buyer isn’t just looking for fancy finishes and great views; they’re expecting a residence that facilitates their lifestyle. In our latest Developer Diary, TRD visited 700 Broadway and sat down with Related California’s Peter Garofalo to learn how the developer created an exclusive luxury residential experience in one of SoCal’s most desirable submarkets. “The ideal resident for 700 Broadway is someone who wants to have it all in a wonderfully designed and luxurious setting,” said Garofalo. “Every space has been perfectly designed to keep a balance between the beachside ease and the city’s bustling energy.” With nearly 70,000 […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Related's 700 Broadway Santa Monica project features 70K sf of amenity space across three buildings.
Amenities include dual rooftop pools, speakeasy bar, sauna barrels; targets luxury resident lifestyle.
Project exemplifies super-prime Santa Monica absorption and high-amenity competition for rent growth.
stories.summary: Related California's 700 Broadway in Santa Monica demonstrates the luxury multifamily trend toward experiential amenities: 70,000 square feet of curated amenity space including rooftop pools, a speakeasy bar, sauna barrels, and private courtyards across three architecturally distinct buildings. The project targets affluent residents seeking beachside ease and urban convenience in one of Southern California's most constrained, expensive markets.
stories.impact: Santa Monica's super-prime segment is absorbing luxury product at scale; this 70K-sf amenity footprint sets new market standards and raises construction and operating costs for competing projects. Developers underwriting Santa Monica, Brentwood, and coastal LA should factor 8–12% of total buildable square footage for high-end amenities and expect lower rents per sf but stronger rent growth and lower turnover on lifestyle-anchored properties.

stories.title: The New Stakeholder in Data Center Development
stories.excerpt: A new stakeholder has entered the data center development process. It does not appear on a cap table. It does not sign a lease. But it is now shaping outcomes as much as capital, land and power. The anti-data center movement is no longer a fringe reaction. It is organized, informed and increasingly effective. Consider Missouri, where organized opposition groups are relatively few, but claim more combined members than any other state in the country — more than triple the next closest. Small in number, enormous in reach. That is not a grassroots reaction. That is an organized constituency operating with real discipline. Suhail Y Tayeb. PHOTO: Courtesy NYU Or consider Utah, where Kevin O’Leary’s Stratos data center project was originally planned to span an area roughly twice the size of Manhattan. After weeks of resident protests and a formal letter from the state Senate president, the company cut its footprint by three quarters and its own leadership admitted the rollout had been mishandled from the start. That is not a story about one developer’s mistake. It is a story about what a coordinated stakeholder can now do to a fully financed project, regardless of its economics. Across the country, projects are being delayed, restructured or canceled not because demand is weak, but because opposition is strong. Local groups are coordinating across states, sharing playbooks, and learning how to influence zoning boards, utility commissions and elections. More than 100 local communities have already enacted their own moratoriums, and they did so independently of one another, without any centralized campaign coordinating the effort.What began as isolated project-level opposition has evolved into a broader network of communities comparing experiences, strategies and outcomes. This is not a temporary phase. It is a structural shift in how infrastructure gets approved. For years, data center development followed a familiar pattern. Secure land, line up power, mo
bullets: Anti-data center opposition now organized, funded, disciplined; shaping development outcomes nationwide.
Missouri has highest organized membership (triple next state); Utah's Stratos project cut scope 75%.
Regulatory risk for data center development rising; entitlement and zoning timelines lengthening materially.
stories.summary: Organized, well-funded opposition to data center development has emerged as a material stakeholder across multiple states, effectively derailing or scaling back projects. Missouri's opposition groups claim membership exceeding triple the next state, and Utah's Stratos project scaled back its footprint by 75% after state Senate intervention. This movement is no longer fringe but disciplined and increasingly effective at slowing or blocking large projects.
stories.impact: Data center opposition is lengthening entitlement timelines and raising political risk in secondary and tertiary markets (Missouri, Utah, etc.). For multifamily developers, this signals: (1) downstream demand risk if data center buildout slows in those markets; (2) opportunity to pivot land banks toward residential in anti-data-center regions; (3) caution on projecting AI/tech job growth without verifying local power/infrastructure approvals are on track.

stories.title: Moratorium on waterfront project applications in downtown West Palm heads to final vote
stories.excerpt: West Palm Beach commissioners will take a final vote on pausing new project applications for high-rise waterfront developments at next week’s meeting.  The proposed ordinance would create a six-month moratorium on new planned development applications for properties south of Monroe Drive, north of Southern Boulevard, west of Flagler Drive and east of Washington Road, according to the commission agenda. Commissioners approved it on first reading at a meeting last week; a second reading vote is scheduled for July 20.  The city cited rapid transformation of that corridor of apartment and condo buildings and is hiring Zyscovich to complete a zoning […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: West Palm Beach commissioners approved six-month moratorium on new waterfront high-rise applications (effective Jan 2027).
12+ luxury condo projects with 2,000+ units in pipeline; Related, Terra, BH Group active.
City cites overdevelopment concerns; hiring Zyscovich for zoning and redevelopment study.
stories.summary: West Palm Beach commissioners approved a six-month moratorium on waterfront high-rise development applications south of Monroe Drive, effective through January 2027, citing rapid overdevelopment. More than a dozen luxury condo projects totaling over 2,000 units are currently in the pipeline or under development, with Miami-based firms (Related, Terra, BH Group) leading activity in the downtown corridor.
stories.impact: West Palm Beach's moratorium signals demand overheating and political pushback on condo-heavy supply; expect entitlement delays for any new waterfront luxury residential projects and potential zoning tightening after the moratorium expires. Multifamily developers should assume tighter approval windows and may see opportunities in mixed-use (retail/office/hotel) projects that diversify use and reduce neighborhood opposition to residential density.

stories.title: MidPen floats 110-unit affordable housing project on former Wells Fargo site in Sunnyvale
stories.excerpt: Developer interest in downtown Sunnyvale continues with another multifamily project proposal as more tech and artificial intelligence workers flock to the Silicon Valley city.  Foster City-based nonprofit MidPen Housing is planning a 110-unit affordable housing building at 295 South Mathilda Avenue in Sunnyvale, the East Bay Times reported. All of the units, save for a market-rate manager’s unit, will be income restricted.  The development was proposed at a former Wells Fargo bank site, which would give way to the residential building. The development earned support from some residents and community groups who claim it “will assist the city by better […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: MidPen Housing proposing 110-unit affordable at former Wells Fargo site in Sunnyvale; 25% for formerly unhoused.
AI/tech migration from SF to Silicon Valley driving multifamily investor interest in Sunnyvale.
Beam Reach also proposing 265-unit conversion of Google office to residential; concurrent pipeline growth.
stories.summary: Foster City nonprofit MidPen Housing is developing a 110-unit affordable housing project at 295 South Mathilda Avenue in Sunnyvale on a former Wells Fargo bank site. The site selection reflects broader multifamily investor pivot to Sunnyvale as AI and tech workers relocate from San Francisco, with concurrent proposals including Beam Reach's 265-unit conversion of a former Google office into residential.
stories.impact: Sunnyvale is emerging as a primary demand driver for Silicon Valley multifamily development, fueled by AI job spillover from San Francisco. Developers should prioritize Sunnyvale infill sites (former office, bank locations) for affordable and market-rate conversion; office-to-residential regulatory pathways are opening, and rents are accelerating as SF constraints push workers south. This is a 24–36 month window before site scarcity and land costs rise sharply.

stories.title: Flipping the Script on “Safe” Haven Bonds
stories.excerpt: Executive Summary The war in Iran has scrambled traditional views of safety in fixed income, pushing investors out of long‑duration Treasuries and broad investment‑grade benchmarks and into floating‑rate, senior‑secured credit. From February 28 through early July, the Invesco Senior Loan ETF (BKLN) returned just over 3% on a total‑return basis, compared with losses of about 5% to 6% for long‑Treasury proxies such as the iShares 20+ Year Treasury Bond ETF (TLT) and mildly negative or flat performance for core bond indexes. War Reshapes “Safe” Bonds A sector‑level review using liquid ETFs shows the divergence clearly. Since the attacks began on February 28, BKLN’s price and income have produced a total return slightly above 3%, more than double the roughly 1.5% gains in dedicated floating‑rate note strategies such as FLRN and short‑maturity high‑yield funds like SJNK over the same window. Long‑duration Treasuries, by contrast, have fallen around 5% in price terms, as represented by TLT’s performance. Core, investment‑grade exposure, proxied by Vanguard Total Bond Market ETF (BND), has also been modestly negative as rising yields offset coupon income. These moves map closely to changing macro expectations. Market‑implied inflation breakevens and policy‑rate futures have shifted toward a higher‑for‑longer profile since late February, while concerns about rising U.S. federal debt levels have intensified, collectively undermining the traditional safe‑haven role of long Treasuries and boosting demand for floating‑rate credit. Why Floating-Rate Credit Is Winning BKLN holds floating‑rate, senior‑secured loans rated below investment grade and currently offers a trailing 12‑month distribution yield in the mid‑single digits, with a portfolio duration close to zero because coupons reset with short‑term benchmarks. In the current environment, investors appear willing to accept additional credit risk in exchange for coupons that adjust upward if the Fed hikes rates, preserving rea
bullets: Floating-rate senior loans (BKLN) returned 3% Feb–July 2026; long Treasuries down 5%, core bonds negative.
Rising federal debt and higher-for-longer rate expectations are shifting capital away from Treasuries.
Senior secured credit now outperforming traditional safe havens; portfolio duration near zero.
stories.summary: Floating-rate, senior-secured credit has significantly outperformed long-duration Treasuries and core investment-grade bonds since late February 2026, returning 3% versus losses of 5–6% for long Treasuries. This shift reflects market expectations of persistently higher interest rates and elevated federal debt concerns, pushing capital toward floating-rate structures with near-zero duration.
stories.impact: Floating-rate credit outperformance signals sustained higher cost of capital for fixed-rate debt and refinancing headwinds for multifamily borrowers. Developers should expect: (1) CMBS and debt funds demanding floating-rate coupons on new originations; (2) pressure on fixed-rate pricing and availability; (3) acceleration of rate-hedging strategies and shorter loan tenors. This favors balance-sheet strong operators and those with floating-rate tolerance, and may slow smaller/marginal project financing.

stories.title: CPC Mortgage Company Secures $42M to Rehabilitate Michigan Housing Property
stories.excerpt: CPC Mortgage Company, a subsidiary of The Community Preservation Corporation, has structured a $42.1 million loan for the preservation and rehabilitation of Parkside Estates and Demby Terraces, a 315-unit affordable housing community in Inkster, Michigan. Developed by Good Housing Partnership in partnership with the Inkster Housing Commission, the properties will undergo a full rehabilitation and conversion under the U.S. Department of Housing and Urban Development’s Rental Assistance Demonstration program, transitioning from public housing to Section 8. CPC Mortgage Company provided the permanent financing through a Freddie Mac Targeted Affordable Housing forward commitment structured as a tax-exempt loan. The financing structure includes Huntington Bank, which served as the lead arranger and agent of the construction loan, with Deutsche Bank and Associated Bank as co-lenders. Cinnaire acted as the Low-Income Housing Tax Credit syndicator. The rehabilitation will include extensive upgrades to both the site and residential units. Join the industry’s top multifamily decision-makers at Connect Apartments on September 22 in L.A. Hear from the investors, developers, lenders, owners, and advisors shaping today’s apartment market through candid conversations, networking, and market insights. Register today at www.connectapts2026.com. The post CPC Mortgage Company Secures $42M to Rehabilitate Michigan Housing Property appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: CPC Mortgage secured $42.1M for 315-unit affordable preservation in Inkster, Michigan via Freddie Mac forward commitment.
Financing includes tax-exempt bonds, Low-Income Housing Tax Credits; RAD program conversion from public housing.
Huntington Bank, Deutsche Bank, Associated Bank as construction lenders; Cinnaire as LIHTC syndicator.
stories.summary: CPC Mortgage Company provided $42.1 million in permanent financing for Parkside Estates and Demby Terraces, a 315-unit affordable housing community in Inkster, Michigan, structured through a Freddie Mac Targeted Affordable Housing forward commitment. The development is converting from public housing to Section 8 under HUD's Rental Assistance Demonstration program, with financing supported by Huntington Bank, Deutsche Bank, and Associated Bank as construction lenders.
stories.impact: This transaction exemplifies stable financing availability for affordable preservation and RAD conversions via Freddie Mac forward commitments and LIHTC structures. Developers with affordable/workforce housing assets should prioritize RAD conversions and Freddie Mac programs as de facto preferred lenders; this signals public-agency partnerships and tax-credit syndicators remain active. Useful benchmark for permanent financing on stabilized, rent-restricted properties.

stories.title: Ice Cream Franchise Coming to Bleecker Street with First NYC Location
stories.excerpt: Scoop N Scootery, a rapidly expanding ice cream franchise with 10 locations across seven states, has selected Bleecker Street for its first New York City location. Lee & Associates NYC arranged the 1,325-square-foot retail lease at 162 Bleecker St., where the company signed a 15-year lease for the ground-floor storefront and plans to open in September 2026. Mark Kapnick, principal at Lee & Associates NYC along with associate director Jordan Cohen, represented landlord Acram Group. Lucas Kooyman of AMPS represented Scoop N Scootery. “New York City is a milestone market for any growing retail brand, and there’s no better place to make that debut than Bleecker Street,” said Kapnick. “Scoop N Scootery wanted a location that immediately establishes its presence in one of the country’s most recognized retail corridors. With exceptional foot traffic, an outstanding mix of food-and-beverage operators and very little available space, Bleecker Street checked every box.” The post Ice Cream Franchise Coming to Bleecker Street with First NYC Location appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Scoop N Scootery signed 15-year lease for 1,325 sf retail at 162 Bleecker St, NYC; opens Sept 2026.
Ice cream franchise expanding from 10 locations across 7 states; Bleecker Street as flagship.
Asking rent $75/sf; minimal retail availability on Bleecker; strong foot traffic anchoring lease.
stories.summary: Scoop N Scootery, a growing ice cream franchise with 10 locations across seven states, signed a 15-year lease for 1,325 square feet at 162 Bleecker Street in Manhattan, planning to open in September 2026. The $75/sf asking rent reflects Bleecker Street's status as a high-traffic, retail-constrained corridor with limited available space.
stories.impact: Minimal relevance to multifamily development. Retail momentum on Bleecker indicates strong foot-traffic economics in lower Manhattan but does not signal demand drivers or supply/cost factors material to apartment development, financing, or capital allocation.

stories.title: Pair of Frisco entrepreneurs ordered to pay $400M in damages to Georgetown business over alleged fraud scheme
stories.excerpt: Frisco entrepreneurs Gopala Krishnan and Sakthivel Gounder were ordered to pay $400 million in damages to a Georgetown-based business after being found liable for breach of contract and fraud by a Williamson County jury.  The pair concocted promotional materials for a luxury development called “The District” that they claimed was going to be built around the San Gabriel River. Krishnan and Gounder were sold the land by the company that sued them, Energy Commissioning Incorporated, owned by Marshall Hussain.  Energy Commissioning was subsequently hired by the pair for $21 million to build necessary infrastructure for a 400-acre, luxury waterfront development […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Frisco entrepreneurs Krishnan, Gounder ordered to pay $400M in fraud damages over 'The District' development scheme.
Pair swindled Georgetown business (Energy Commissioning) out of $21M; promised 400-acre waterfront luxury development.
Scheme involved misrepresenting project scale to European bankers; pair previously targeted by SEC.
stories.summary: Frisco-based entrepreneurs Gopala Krishnan and Sakthivel Gounder were ordered by a Williamson County jury to pay $400 million in damages for fraudulently promoting a luxury waterfront development called 'The District' that was never intended to be built. The pair defrauded Energy Commissioning Inc. of $21 million under the guise of infrastructure development for a 400-acre project around the San Gabriel River, and had previously been the subject of SEC enforcement action.
stories.impact: Minimal relevance to multifamily development. This is a fraud/litigation case involving spec development and alleged Ponzi scheme activity. No demand, supply, capital, or cost signals for apartment markets; useful only as caution on due diligence and partner vetting in distressed/speculative land development.

stories.title: Bellhops and bass fishing: Wildhaven developer bets big on nature-centric luxury in Denton
stories.excerpt: A planned luxury resort might be coming to Denton, complete with six miles of horse trails to help bring residents and visitors closer to nature.  Sarasota, Florida-based An Ki Land Development’s managing director, Logan Breen, told the Dallas Business Journal that the new resort they plan on building is intended to combine upscale homes and hotels with “authentic outdoor experiences,” with a total value between $350 and $450 million when completed. The resort, called Wildhaven, is set to span 740 acres on the north side of Denton. 152 homes are set to be built in the resort, ranging from $1.5 […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: An Ki Land Development planning 740-acre Wildhaven resort in Denton, TX; $350–450M total value.
152 homes ($1.5–3M+), 100-key hotel, equestrian, spa, 10-acre bass pond; 400 of 740 acres developed.
Denton selected for first location due to strong population growth, development activity in DFW region.
stories.summary: Sarasota-based An Ki Land Development is planning Wildhaven, a 740-acre luxury master-planned resort in Denton, Texas, with $350–450 million total value. The project includes 152 luxury homes ($1.5–3M+), a 100-key hotel, equestrian facilities, spa, and recreational amenities across 400 developed acres, with 300+ acres preserved. Noble Classic Homes is building phase-one homes; HKS is the design architect.
stories.impact: Denton's selection as first Wildhaven location reflects DFW's rapid population growth and strong demand for high-amenity lifestyle projects. Developers should track Denton rental/for-sale absorption rates as an indicator of housing demand migration within DFW; strong luxury resort demand here may signal demand overflow from Dallas/Frisco submarkets and timing for apartment infill in emerging Denton corridors.

stories.title: Shops at Sunset Place owner seeks taxing district to raise $149M
stories.excerpt: The long-awaited redevelopment of the Shops at Sunset Place in South Miami is seeking a nine-figure boost for the mixed-use project’s infrastructure.  Midtown Development, led by founder Alex Vadia, is requesting Miami-Dade County’s approval to establish a special taxing district that would finance $149 million in infrastructure for its planned 10.2-acre Downtown SoMi mixed-use redevelopment at 5701–5795 Southwest 72nd Street.  The developer proposed creating the Downtown SoMi Community Development District, a special-purpose governmental entity that can finance infrastructure and oversee its maintenance within the development. County commissioners are scheduled to consider the proposal during their July 21 meeting.  The district […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Midtown Development seeking $149M special taxing district for Downtown SoMi mixed-use redevelopment in South Miami.
Project: 1,513 residential units, 200K+ sf retail, 287 hotel rooms, 287-seat theater, 65.9K sf office.
Infrastructure bonds to fund roads, utilities, parking; construction targeted to start 2027, wrap Q1 2029.
stories.summary: Midtown Development (led by Alex Vadia) is requesting Miami-Dade County approval for a special Community Development District to issue $149 million in tax-exempt bonds for infrastructure on its 10.2-acre Downtown SoMi mixed-use redevelopment at the former Shops at Sunset Place. The project includes 1,513 residential units, 200,000+ square feet of retail, 287 hotel rooms, and supporting office and parking; construction is slated for 2027–Q1 2029.
stories.impact: South Miami's 1,513-unit pipeline signals aggressive multifamily supply growth in a mature Miami-Dade submarket; the special taxing district structure allows private capture of infrastructure costs, de-risking the developer. Competitors should map Downtown SoMi's absorption trajectory against broader Miami-Dade supply pipeline; expect rental rate pressure if leasing velocity doesn't match deliveries. Monitor CDD approval and construction timeline as signal of South Miami market fundamentals.

stories.title: Pure Paws Veterinary Care to Relocate to 5K SF at Brooklyn’s 1 Putnam Avenue
stories.excerpt: A pack of Brooklyn veterinarians are taking themselves for a short walk. Pure Paws Veterinary Care is relocating its Brooklyn practice to a larger, nearby location in the Clinton Hill neighborhood. The veterinary office signed a 5,000-square-foot lease at 1 Putnam Avenue, near the intersection of Cambridge Place and Fulton Street. The asking rent for the 10-year lease, signed July 1, was $75 per square foot. Traded first reported the deal. The mixed-use building’s landlord is Abe Betesh. Gabriel and Solomon Betesh of commercial brokerage Momentum Realty Group negotiated the deal on the landlord’s behalf. “We are excited to welcome Pure Paws Veterinary to a new and expanded site to service Clinton Hill,” the brokers said in a joint statement. “We believe in the continued growth of the neighborhood and are proud to play a role in strengthening and enhancing its retail landscape by bringing high-quality businesses to the community.” Pure Paws Veterinary Care currently operates three New York City locations, at 506 West 42nd Street in Manhattan’s Hell’s Kitchen, at 350 Hudson Street in Hudson Square and at 944 Fulton Street in Brooklyn’s Clinton Hill, according to its website. It is unclear when the practice will relocate from its current Clinton Hill address, which is less than a block from its future location.. Babson Real Estate Advisors‘ Richard Bradbury represented the practice in the lease negotiations. Bradbury told Commercial Observer in a statement that Babson was pleased to represent the Pure Paws. “It will give them the opportunity to better serve their loyal and growing customer base for years to come,” Babson said. A representative for Pure Paws did not immediately respond to a request for comment. Emily Davis can be reached at edavis@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Pure Paws Veterinary Care signed 5,000 sf lease at 1 Putnam Avenue, Brooklyn Clinton Hill; $75/sf.
10-year term; veterinary tenant relocating to larger, nearby location in same neighborhood.
Continued healthcare/professional services anchoring retail in Clinton Hill; mixed-use building landlord is Abe Betesh.
stories.summary: Pure Paws Veterinary Care signed a 5,000-square-foot lease at 1 Putnam Avenue in Clinton Hill, Brooklyn, at $75 per square foot on a 10-year term. The practice is relocating from elsewhere in Brooklyn to this larger location near Cambridge Place and Fulton Street, with the deal signed July 1.
stories.impact: Minimal relevance to multifamily development. Retail leasing (veterinary services) in Clinton Hill signals stable neighborhood demand but does not indicate multifamily demand drivers, supply, capital, or cost factors material to apartment development. Only relevant as neighborhood stability indicator.

stories.title: Bridges Development Group Buys Kingswood Center in Brooklyn for $31M
stories.excerpt: Kingswood Center in Brooklyn’s Midwood neighborhood has a new owner, Commercial Observer has learned. The 229,926-square-foot shopping center at 1630 East 15th Street sold for $31 million to Michael Berfield’s Bridges Development Group, according to broker JLL. The sale marks a steep discount from the $89 million that the shopping center fetched in early 2020, the last time it traded. The three-story, Class B property is currently anchored by T.J. Maxx. A LoopNet listing advertises nearly 100,000 square feet of retail and commercial space available for lease. Kingswood Center was constructed in 2007 and renovated in 2019, according to the listing, and features an on-site parking garage. JLL’s Ethan Stanton, Michael Mazzara and Brendan Maddigan represented the seller, PNC’s Midland Loan Services, in direct negotiations with the buyer. A JLL debt team of Scott Aiese and Alex Staikos secured $34.65 million in acquisition financing for the buyer. The firm declined to disclose the lender. The financing will allow Bridges Development Group to execute a business plan of investing in the property and leasing its vacant space, according to a JLL spokesperson. Midland took possession of the property from its former owner, retail shopping REIT Urban Edge Properties, in 2023. The REIT fell behind on a $66 million commercial mortgage-backed securities (CMBS) loan used to acquire the center in February 2020. Following the ill-timed purchase, the shopping center’s annual net operating income fell to $1.9 million and occupancy declined to 35 percent, The Real Deal reported at the time. Urban Edge Properties still owns the nearby Kingswood Crossing at 1715 East 13th Street, according to its website. Spokespeople for Bridges Development Group and PNC did not immediately respond to requests for comment. Emily Davis can be reached at edavis@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Bridges Development Group acquired 229,926 sf Kingswood Center in Midwood, Brooklyn for $31M.
Shopping center sold at 65% discount from 2020 price ($89M); T.J. Maxx anchor; 100K sf vacant.
JLL arranged $34.65M acquisition financing (lender undisclosed); property constructed 2007, renovated 2019.
stories.summary: Michael Berfield's Bridges Development Group purchased Kingswood Center, a 229,926-square-foot shopping center in Brooklyn's Midwood neighborhood, for $31 million—a steep 65% discount from its $89 million sale price in early 2020. The Class B property is anchored by T.J. Maxx and features nearly 100,000 square feet of vacant retail space. JLL arranged $34.65 million in acquisition financing.
stories.impact: Brooklyn retail distress (Kingswood's 65% value decline from 2020) signals legacy retail asset weakness, but Bridges Development Group's acquisition suggests repositioning intent (likely mixed-use conversion or operator consolidation). Multifamily developers should monitor for conversion-to-residential opportunities in Brooklyn Midwood if retail lease-up stalls; retail-to-residential conversions may offer land cost arbitrage vs. greenfield development.

stories.title: Data Intelligence Firm Curinos Takes 14K SF at Marx Realty’s 10 Grand Central
stories.excerpt: Curinos, a global data intelligence firm serving the financial industry, is relocating to Marx Realty’s 10 Grand Central after signing a 14,000-square-foot lease, Commercial Observer has learned. Curinos will move from its current office about two blocks north at 485 Lexington Avenue, though it is unclear when the move will happen. The length of the lease was not disclosed. Asking rents at 10 Grand Central — which is now 98 percent leased, according to the landlord — ranges from $85 to $135 per square foot. Adam Ardise and Rachel Rosenfeld from Cushman & Wakefield represented Curinos in the lease, while JLL’s Mitchell Konsker, Thomas Swartz, Carlee Palmer and Margaux Kelleher represented Marx Realty. Spokespeople for C&W and JLL did not immediately respond to CO’s requests for comment. “The robust leasing activity at 10 Grand Central demonstrates the ongoing appeal of thoughtfully designed workplaces and white glove service that combine to give companies an elevated office experience,” Craig Deitelzweig, CEO of Marx Realty, said in a statement. “Curinos’ decision to relocate within the Grand Central market speaks to the differentiated hospitality-infused experience we’ve created at 10 Grand Central and further strengthens the building’s exceptional tenant roster,” Deitelzweig added. 10 Grand Central is a hospitality-infused office building that offers tenants a variety of amenities, including a reservable house car for transportation, a lounge and cafe on the seventh floor, a 1930s French garden-style outdoor terrace, and a newly opened speakeasy-style restaurant called Highball Ltd. Other tenants at 10 Grand Central include data center developer Edged, bank holding company Merchants Bancorp and research firm Green Street Advisors. Amanda Schiavo can be reached at aschiavo@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Curinos (data intelligence firm) leased 14,000 sf at Marx Realty's 10 Grand Central, NYC office.
Building now 98% leased; rents range $85–135/sf; Curinos relocating from 485 Lexington Ave.
Strong office leasing momentum at Grand Central reflecting financial-sector tenant demand.
stories.summary: Curinos, a global data intelligence firm serving financial services, signed a 14,000-square-foot lease at Marx Realty's 10 Grand Central in Manhattan (lease signed, move timing unclear). The building is now 98% percent leased with rents ranging $85–135 per square foot. Curinos is relocating from 485 Lexington Avenue, approximately two blocks north.
stories.impact: Minimal relevance to multifamily development. Office leasing activity at Grand Central indicates selective tenant demand in Manhattan but does not signal multifamily demand, supply, capital, or cost factors. Useful only as indicator of financial-sector stability in Manhattan; does not materially affect apartment markets or development.

stories.title: Iris Escarrá of Greenberg Traurig: 5 Questions
stories.excerpt: As co-chair of the land use practice at law firm Greenberg Traurig, Iris Escarrá has worked on a number of high-profile projects. In one recent example, Escarrá was part of the team that won approvals for Miami Freedom Park, the mixed-use development near Miami International Airport that includes Nu Stadium, the 26,700-seat home of Major League Soccer’s Inter Miami CF. Before the stadium opened in April, she led the team that guided the development team through environmental remediation, code compliance and traffic and aviation issues. The following conversation has been edited for length and clarity.   Commercial Observer: Now that Nu Stadium is open and Inter Miami is playing matches there, what lessons did you learn from the project? Iris Escarrá: There’s so many. Some of them I have to cover over drinks. Some of them I can cover here. The stadium is actually one component. It’s 12 acres of a 73-acre complex that’s mixed-use. So while the stadium is the biggest to come in, we have retail coming in, hotel, office, and so forth that we’re also working on. It’s very exciting because that’s what’s actually going to create the whole district. Inter Miami has 17 home games, and they’ll have other events there, concerts, soccer games for high schools, but it’s never going to be a 365-day-a-year building. So it’s really the other uses around it that are going to create the district. And those uses around it are actually what helps with some of the traffic items because the restaurants and the retail are open a few hours before the match, so if you want to go and have lunch before the game, you could do that. If you want to go after the game, go hang out. So what happens is that the mixed-use district around the stadiums is what’s really creating the whole district. When are those other uses going to be completed? We have a few buildings that are in for permitting now, some food and beverage, some entertainment. T
bullets: Iris Escarrá (Greenberg Traurig) led approvals for Miami Freedom Park: 73-acre mixed-use, Nu Stadium (MLS).
Nu Stadium (26,700-seat Inter Miami CF venue) opened April 2026; stadium is one component of complex.
Project includes retail, hotel, office alongside stadium; navigated environmental, code, traffic, aviation issues.
stories.summary: Iris Escarrá, co-chair of land use at law firm Greenberg Traurig, led the approvals team for Miami Freedom Park, a 73-acre mixed-use development near Miami International Airport featuring Nu Stadium (26,700-seat Inter Miami CF venue, opened April 2026), plus retail, hotel, office, and other components. The project required navigation of environmental remediation, code compliance, and traffic/aviation issues.
stories.impact: Minimal relevance to multifamily development. This is a profile of a land-use attorney and an MLS stadium complex approval case. While the project is mixed-use, the primary asset is the sports venue; no direct signals on apartment demand, supply, or capital in Miami beyond the broader development activity indicator. Useful for understanding entitlement complexity on large projects but not actionable for multifamily underwriting.
